problem.title: Isomorphic Strings
problem.description:
Given two strings s and t, determine if they are isomorphic.
                
Definition: Two strings s and t are isomorphic if the characters in s can be replaced to get t.
                
Rules:

All occurrences of a character must be replaced with another character
No two characters may map to the same character
A character may map to itself

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Two Hash Maps
solutions.description: Maintain two hash maps — one mapping characters from s to t and one in the reverse direction — and verify at each position that both mappings are consistent with what has been seen before. If either map already contains a character mapped to a different target, the strings cannot be isomorphic. The key insight is that isomorphism requires a bijection, so both the forward and backward mappings must be checked simultaneously.

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Array-based Mapping
solutions.description: Replace the hash maps with two fixed-size integer arrays of length 256 (one per ASCII character), initialized to -1 to indicate no mapping yet. At each position, check that both arrays agree on the current character pair; if they conflict, return false. The key insight is that arrays provide O(1) access with lower constant overhead than hash maps, making this approach faster in practice while still O(n) in theory.

complexity.space: O(1) since arrays are fixed size

solutions.title: Solution 3 – Single Pass with Last Occurrence
solutions.description: Track the last index at which each character appeared in s and t respectively, then compare these indices at every position. Two characters are in the same equivalence class only if they always appear at the same positions, so mismatched last-seen indices reveal a mapping conflict without maintaining explicit character-to-character maps. The key insight is that equal last-occurrence patterns are equivalent to a consistent bijection between the two character sets.

solutions.title: Solution 4 – Character Transformation
solutions.description: Normalize both strings independently by replacing each character with the index of its first occurrence, then compare the two normalized forms for equality. Two strings are isomorphic if and only if their normalized forms are identical, because the normalization captures the structural pattern of character repetitions. The key insight is that reducing each string to its pattern of first-occurrence indices creates a canonical form that is independent of the actual characters used.

solutions.title: Edge Cases and Validation
